output: 1 --- 2 title: How to migrate from Pages to the App Router 3 nav_title: App Router 4 description: Learn how to upgrade your existing Next.js application from the Pages Router to the App Router. 5 --- 6 7 This guide will help you: 8 9 - [Update your Next.js application from version 12 to version 13](#nextjs-version) 10 - [Upgrade features that work in both the `pages` and the `app` directories](#upgrading-new-features) 11 - [Incrementally migrate your existing application from `pages` to `app`](#migrating-from-pages-to-app) 12 13 ## Upgrading 14 15 ### Node.js Version 16 17 The minimum Node.js version is now **v18.17**. See the [Node.js documentation](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v18.x/api/) for more information. 18 19 ### Next.js Version 20 21 To update to Next.js version 13, run the following command using your preferred package manager: 22 23 ```bash package="pnpm" 24 pnpm add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 25 ``` 26 27 ```bash package="npm" 28 npm install next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 29 ``` 30 31 ```bash package="yarn" 32 yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 33 ``` 34 35 ```bash package="bun" 36 bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 37 ``` 38 39 ### ESLint Version 40 41 If you're using ESLint, you need to upgrade your ESLint version: 42 43 ```bash package="pnpm" 44 pnpm add -D eslint-config-next@latest 45 ``` 46 47 ```bash package="npm" 48 npm install -D eslint-config-next@latest 49 ``` 50 51 ```bash package="yarn" 52 yarn add -D eslint-config-next@latest 53 ``` 54 55 ```bash package="bun" 56 bun add -D eslint-config-next@latest 57 ``` 58 59 > **Good to know**: You may need to restart the ESLint server in VS Code for the ESLint changes to take effect. Open the Command Palette (`cmd+shift+p` on Mac; `ctrl+shift+p` on Windows) and search for `ESLint: Restart ESLint Server`. 60 61 ## Next Steps 62 63 After you've updated, see the following sections for next steps: 64 65 - [Upgrade new features](#upgrading-new-features): A guide to help you upgrade to new features such as the improved Image and Link Components. 66 - [Migrate from the `pages` to `app` directory](#migrating-from-pages-to-app): A step-by-step guide to help you incrementally migrate from the `pages` to the `app` directory. 67 68 ## Upgrading New Features 69 70 Next.js 13 introduced the new [App Router](/docs/app) with new features and conventions. The new Router is available in the `app` directory and co-exists with the `pages` directory. 71 72 Upgrading to Next.js 13 does **not** require using the App Router. You can continue using `pages` with new features that work in both directories, such as the updated [Image component](#image-component), [Link component](#link-component), [Script component](#script-component), and [Font optimization](#font-optimization). 73 74 ### `` Component 75 76 Next.js 12 introduced new improvements to the Image Component with a temporary import: `next/future/image`. These improvements included less client-side JavaScript, easier ways to extend and style images, better accessibility, and native browser lazy loading. 77 78 In version 13, this new behavior is now the default for `next/image`. 79 80 There are two codemods to help you migrate to the new Image Component: 81 82 - [**`next-image-to-legacy-image` codemod**](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#next-image-to-legacy-image): Safely and automatically renames `next/image` imports to `next/legacy/image`. Existing components will maintain the same behavior. 83 - [**`next-image-experimental` codemod**](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#next-image-experimental): Dangerously adds inline styles and removes unused props. This will change the behavior of existing components to match the new defaults. To use this codemod, you need to run the `next-image-to-legacy-image` codemod first. 84 85 ### `` Component 86 87 The [`` Component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link) no longer requires manually adding an `` tag as a child. This behavior was added as an experimental option in [version 12.2](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-12-2) and is now the default. In Next.js 13, `` always renders `` and allows you to forward props to the underlying tag. 88 89 For example: 90 91 ```jsx 92 import Link from 'next/link' 93 94 // Next.js 12: `` has to be nested otherwise it's excluded 95 96 About 97 98 99 // Next.js 13: `` always renders `` under the hood 100 101 About 102 103 ``` 104 105 To upgrade your links to Next.js 13, you can use the [`new-link` codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#new-link). 106 107 ### `

1 --- 2 title: Layouts and Pages 3 description: Learn how to create your first pages and layouts, and link between them with the Link component. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7 links: 8 - app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating 9 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout 10 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/page 11 - app/api-reference/components/link 12 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes 13 --- 14 15 Next.js uses **file-system based routing**, meaning you can use folders and files to define routes. This page will guide you through how to create layouts and pages, and link between them. 16 17 ## Creating a page 18 19 A **page** is UI that is rendered on a specific route. To create a page, add a [`page` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) inside the `app` directory and default export a React component. For example, to create an index page (`/`): 20 21 28 29 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 30 export default function Page() { 31 return

Hello Next.js!

32 } 33 ``` 34 35 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 36 export default function Page() { 37 return

Hello Next.js!

38 } 39 ``` 40 41 ## Creating a layout 42 43 A layout is UI that is **shared** between multiple pages. On navigation, layouts preserve state, remain interactive, and do not rerender. 44 45 You can define a layout by default exporting a React component from a [`layout` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout). The component should accept a `children` prop which can be a page or another [layout](#nesting-layouts). 46 47 For example, to create a layout that accepts your index page as child, add a `layout` file inside the `app` directory: 48 49 56 57 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 58 export default function DashboardLayout({ 59 children, 60 }: { 61 children: React.ReactNode 62 }) { 63 return ( 64 65 66 {/* Layout UI */} 67 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 68
{children}
69 70 71 ) 72 } 73 ``` 74 75 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 76 export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) { 77 return ( 78 79 80 {/* Layout UI */} 81 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 82
{children}
83 84 85 ) 86 } 87 ``` 88 89 The layout above is called a [root layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#root-layout) because it's defined at the root of the `app` directory. The root layout is **required** and must contain `html` and `body` tags. 90 91 ## Creating a nested route 92 93 A nested route is a route composed of multiple URL segments. For example, the `/blog/[slug]` route is composed of three segments: 94 95 - `/` (Root Segment) 96 - `blog` (Segment) 97 - `[slug]` (Leaf Segment) 98 99 In Next.js: 100 101 - **Folders** are used to define the route segments that map to URL segments. 102 - **Files** (like `page` and `layout`) are used to create UI that is shown for a segment. 103 104 To create nested routes, you can nest folders inside each other. For example, to add a route for `/blog`, create a folder called `blog` in the `app` directory. Then, to make `/blog` publicly accessible, add a `page.tsx` file: 105 106 113 114 ```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" switcher 115 // Dummy imports 116 import { getPosts } from '@/lib/posts' 117 import { Post } from '@/ui/post' 118 119 export default async function Page() { 120 const posts = await getPosts() 121 122 return ( 123

128 ) 129 } 130 ``` 131 132 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 133 // Dummy imports 134 import { getPosts } from '@/lib/posts' 135 import { Post } from '@/ui/post' 136 137 export default async function Page() { 138 const posts = await getPosts() 139 140 return ( 141
    142 {posts.map((post) => ( 143 144 ))} 145
146 ) 147 } 148 ``` 149 150 You can continue nesting folders to create nested routes. For example, to create a route for a specific blog post, create a new `[slug]` folder inside `blog` and add a `page` file: 151 152 159 160 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 161 function generateStaticParams() {} 162 163 export default function Page() { 164 return

Hello, Blog Post Page!

165 } 166 ``` 167 168 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 169 function generateStaticParams() {} 170 171 export default function Page() { 172 return

Hello, Blog Post Page!

173 } 174 ``` 175 176 Wrapping a folder name in square brackets (e.g. `[slug]`) creates a [dynamic route segment](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) which is used to generate multiple pages from data. e.g. blog posts, product pages, etc. 177 178 ## Nesting layouts 179 180 By default, layouts in the folder hierarchy are also nested, which means they wrap child layouts via their `children` prop. You can nest layouts by adding `layout` inside specific route segments (folders). 181 182 For example, to create a layout for the `/blog` route, add a new `layout` file inside the `blog` folder. 183 184 191 192 ```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher 193 export default function BlogLayout({ 194 children, 195 }: { 196 children: React.ReactNode 197 }) { 198 return
{children}
199 } 200 ```

50	## Reference
51	
52	### Props
53	
54	#### `children` (required)
55	
56	Layout components should accept and use a `children` prop. During rendering, `children` will be populated with the route segments the layout is wrapping. These will primarily be the component of a child [Layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) (if it exists) or [Page](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), but could also be other special files like [Loading](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) or [Error](/docs/app/getting-started/error-handling) when applicable.
57	
58	#### `params` (optional)
59	
60	A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) object from the root segment down to that layout.
61	
62	```tsx filename="app/dashboard/[team]/layout.tsx" switcher
63	export default async function Layout({
64	  children,
65	  params,
66	}: {
67	  children: React.ReactNode
68	  params: Promise<{ team: string }>
69	}) {
70	  const { team } = await params
71	}
72	```

77	}
78	```

82	| `app/dashboard/[team]/layout.js`  | `/dashboard/1` | `Promise<{ team: '1' }>`           |
83	| `app/shop/[tag]/[item]/layout.js` | `/shop/1/2`    | `Promise<{ tag: '1', item: '2' }>` |

86	- Since the `params` prop is a promise. You must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values.
87	  - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future.

5	
6	The `generateStaticParams` function can be used in combination with [dynamic route segments](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) to [**statically generate**](/docs/app/guides/caching#static-rendering) routes at build time instead of on-demand at request time.
7	
8	`generateStaticParams` can be used with:
9	
10	- [Pages](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) (`page.tsx`/`page.js`)
11	- [Layouts](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) (`layout.tsx`/`layout.js`)
12	- [Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) (`route.ts`/`route.js`)
13	
14	```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher
15	// Return a list of `params` to populate the [slug] dynamic segment
16	export async function generateStaticParams() {
17	  const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json())
18	
19	  return posts.map((post) => ({
20	    slug: post.slug,
21	  }))
22	}
23	
24	// Multiple versions of this page will be statically generated
25	// using the `params` returned by `generateStaticParams`
26	export default async function Page({
27	  params,
28	}: {
29	  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>
30	}) {
31	  const { slug } = await params
32	  // ...
33	}
34	```
35	
36	```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher
37	// Return a list of `params` to populate the [slug] dynamic segment
38	export async function generateStaticParams() {
39	  const posts = await fetch('https://.../posts').then((res) => res.json())
40	
41	  return posts.map((post) => ({
42	    slug: post.slug,
43	  }))
44	}
45	
46	// Multiple versions of this page will be statically generated
47	// using the `params` returned by `generateStaticParams`

53	
54	> **Good to know**:
55	>
56	> - You can use the [`dynamicParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#dynamicparams) segment config option to control what happens when a dynamic segment is visited that was not generated with `generateStaticParams`.
57	> - You must return [an empty array from `generateStaticParams`](#all-paths-at-build-time) or utilize [`export const dynamic = 'force-static'`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#dynamic) in order to revalidate (ISR) [paths at runtime](#all-paths-at-runtime).
58	> - During `next dev`, `generateStaticParams` will be called when you navigate to a route.
59	> - During `next build`, `generateStaticParams` runs before the corresponding Layouts or Pages are generated.
60	> - During revalidation (ISR), `generateStaticParams` will not be called again.
61	> - `generateStaticParams` replaces the [`getStaticPaths`](/docs/pages/api-reference/functions/get-static-paths) function in the Pages Router.

64	
65	`options.params` (optional)

75	- Each property in the object is a dynamic segment to be filled in for the route.

17	---
18	
19	The Metadata APIs can be used to define your application metadata for improved SEO and web shareability and include:
20	
21	1. [The static `metadata` object](#static-metadata)
22	2. [The dynamic `generateMetadata` function](#generated-metadata)
23	3. Special [file conventions](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata) that can be used to add static or dynamically generated [favicons](#favicons) and [OG images](#static-open-graph-images).
24	
25	With all the options above, Next.js will automatically generate the relevant `<head>` tags for your page, which can be inspected in the browser's developer tools.
26	
27	The `metadata` object and `generateMetadata` function exports are only supported in Server Components.
28	
29	## Default fields
30	
31	There are two default `meta` tags that are always added even if a route doesn't define metadata:
32	
33	- The [meta charset tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Element/meta#attr-charset) sets the character encoding for the website.
34	- The [meta viewport tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Viewport_meta_tag) sets the viewport width and scale for the website to adjust for different devices.
35	
36	```html
37	<meta charset="utf-8" />
38	<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
39	```
40	
41	The other metadata fields can be defined with the `Metadata` object (for [static metadata](#static-metadata)) or the `generateMetadata` function (for [generated metadata](#generated-metadata)).
42	
43	## Static metadata
44	
45	To define static metadata, export a [`Metadata` object](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-object) from a static [`layout.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) or [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) file. For example, to add a title and description to the blog route:
46	
47	```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher
48	import type { Metadata } from 'next'
49	
50	export const metadata: Metadata = {
51	  title: 'My Blog',
52	  description: '...',
53	}
54	
55	export default function Layout() {}
56	```
57	
58	```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher
59	export const metadata = {
60	  title: 'My Blog',
61	  description: '...',
62	}
63	
64	export default function Layout() {}
65	```
66	
67	You can view a full list of available options, in the [`generateMetadata` documentation](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-fields).
68	
69	## Generated metadata
70	
71	You can use [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata) function to `fetch` metadata that depends on data. For example, to fetch the title and description for a specific blog post:
72	
73	```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher
74	import type { Metadata, ResolvingMetadata } from 'next'
75	
76	type Props = {
77	  params: Promise<{ slug: string }>
78	  searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }>
79	}
80	
81	export async function generateMetadata(
82	  { params, searchParams }: Props,
83	  parent: ResolvingMetadata
84	): Promise<Metadata> {
85	  const slug = (await params).slug
86	
87	  // fetch post information
88	  const post = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${slug}`).then((res) =>
89	    res.json()
90	  )
91	
92	  return {
93	    title: post.title,
94	    description: post.description,

Now I have a clear picture. Let me plan the migration:

**Current Pages Router files and their App Router equivalents:**

1. `pages/_app.js` → `app/layout.tsx` (root layout with header/nav/footer + AppProvider)
2. `pages/_document.js` → Merged into `app/layout.tsx` (html/body tags, fonts, meta)
3. `pages/_error.js` → `app/error.tsx` (error boundary)
4. `pages/404.js` → `app/not-found.tsx` (404 page)
5. `pages/index.js` → `app/page.tsx` (home page with getServerSideProps → async server component + fetch)
6. `pages/blog/index.js` → `app/blog/page.tsx` (blog index with getStaticProps → async server component + fetch with revalidation)
7. `pages/blog/[id].js` → `app/blog/[id]/page.tsx` (blog post with getStaticPaths → generateStaticParams, getStaticProps → async server component)

**Key changes needed:**

1. **Data fetching**: `getServerSideProps` → async server component with direct `fetch`. `getStaticProps` → async server component. `getStaticPaths` → `generateStaticParams`.
2. **Metadata**: `next/head` with `<Head>` → `export const metadata` or `generateMetadata` function
3. **Router**: `useRouter` for navigation → `Link` component or `useRouter` from `next/navigation`
4. **CSS**: Global CSS import moves from `_app.js` to root `layout.tsx`
5. **Providers**: AppProvider needs `'use client'` directive and becomes a client component wrapper
6. **Error page**: `_error.js` with `getInitialProps` → `error.tsx` (client component)
7. **404 page**: `pages/404.js` → `app/not-found.tsx`

Let me also check what `useRouter` from `next/navigation` provides — since `router.back()` and `router.push()` are used.

For the App Router:
- `useRouter` from `next/navigation` provides: `back()`, `forward()`, `refresh()`, `push()`, `replace()`, `prefetch()`
- No `router.isFallback` — instead we handle loading states with `loading.tsx` or the `useParams` hook

For the blog post page:
- `router.isFallback` → In App Router, `fallback: 'blocking'` behavior is default for dynamic params not generated, but we don't need a loading check. Instead we use `loading.tsx` or use `useParams` with proper handling.
- Actually, `generateStaticParams` with `export const dynamicParams = true` (default) is equivalent to `fallback: true`. For `fallback: 'blocking'`, we still just leave it as default.

23	
24	## Options
25	
26	### `dynamic`
27	
28	Change the dynamic behavior of a layout or page to fully static or fully dynamic.
29	
30	```tsx filename="layout.tsx | page.tsx | route.ts" switcher
31	export const dynamic = 'auto'
32	// 'auto' | 'force-dynamic' | 'error' | 'force-static'
33	```
34	
35	```js filename="layout.js | page.js | route.js" switcher
36	export const dynamic = 'auto'
37	// 'auto' | 'force-dynamic' | 'error' | 'force-static'
38	```
39	
40	> **Good to know**: The new model in the `app` directory favors granular caching control at the `fetch` request level over the binary all-or-nothing model of `getServerSideProps` and `getStaticProps` at the page-level in the `pages` directory. The `dynamic` option is a way to opt back in to the previous model as a convenience and provides a simpler migration path.
41	
42	- **`'auto'`** (default): The default option to cache as much as possible without preventing any components from opting into dynamic behavior.
43	- **`'force-dynamic'`**: Force [dynamic rendering](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering), which will result in routes being rendered for each user at request time. This option is equivalent to:
44	  - Setting the option of every `fetch()` request in a layout or page to `{ cache: 'no-store', next: { revalidate: 0 } }`.
45	  - Setting the segment config to `export const fetchCache = 'force-no-store'`
46	
47	- **`'error'`**: Force static rendering and cache the data of a layout or page by causing an error if any components use [Dynamic APIs](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering) or uncached data. This option is equivalent to:
48	  - `getStaticProps()` in the `pages` directory.
49	  - Setting the option of every `fetch()` request in a layout or page to `{ cache: 'force-cache' }`.
50	  - Setting the segment config to `fetchCache = 'only-cache'`.
51	- **`'force-static'`**: Force static rendering and cache the data of a layout or page by forcing [`cookies`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies), [`headers()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers) and [`useSearchParams()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params) to return empty values. It is possible to [`revalidate`](#revalidate), [`revalidatePath`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidatePath), or [`revalidateTag`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag), in pages or layouts rendered with `force-static`.
52	
53	> **Good to know**:
54	>
55	> - Instructions on [how to migrate](/docs/app/guides/migrating/app-router-migration#step-6-migrating-data-fetching-methods) from `getServerSideProps` and `getStaticProps` to `dynamic: 'force-dynamic'` and `dynamic: 'error'` can be found in the [upgrade guide](/docs/app/guides/migrating/app-router-migration#step-6-migrating-data-fetching-methods).
56	
57	### `dynamicParams`
58	
59	Control what happens when a dynamic segment is visited that was not generated with [generateStaticParams](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params).
60	
61	```tsx filename="layout.tsx | page.tsx" switcher
62	export const dynamicParams = true // true | false
63	```
64	
65	```js filename="layout.js | page.js | route.js" switcher
66	export const dynamicParams = true // true | false
67	```
68	
69	- **`true`** (default): Dynamic segments not included in `generateStaticParams` are generated on demand.
70	- **`false`**: Dynamic segments not included in `generateStaticParams` will return a 404.
71	
72	> **Good to know**:
73	>
74	> - This option replaces the `fallback: true | false | blocking` option of `getStaticPaths` in the `pages` directory.
75	> - To statically render all paths the first time they're visited, you'll need to return an empty array in `generateStaticParams` or utilize `export const dynamic = 'force-static'`.
76	> - When `dynamicParams = true`, the segment uses [Streaming Server Rendering](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#streaming).
77	
78	### `revalidate`
79	
80	Set the default revalidation time for a layout or page. This option does not override the `revalidate` value set by individual `fetch` requests.
81	
82	```tsx filename="layout.tsx | page.tsx | route.ts" switcher
83	export const revalidate = false
84	// false | 0 | number
85	```
86	
87	```js filename="layout.js | page.js | route.js" switcher
88	export const revalidate = false
89	// false | 0 | number
90	```
91	
92	- **`false`** (default): The default heuristic to cache any `fetch` requests that set their `cache` option to `'force-cache'` or are discovered before a [Dynamic API](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering) is used. Semantically equivalent to `revalidate: Infinity` which effectively means the resource should be cached indefinitely. It is still possible for individual `fetch` requests to use `cache: 'no-store'` or `revalidate: 0` to avoid being cached and make the route dynamically rendered. Or set `revalidate` to a positive number lower than the route default to increase the revalidation frequency of a route.
93	- **`0`**: Ensure a layout or page is always [dynamically rendered](/docs/app/guides/caching#dynamic-rendering) even if no Dynamic APIs or uncached data fetches are discovered. This option changes the default of `fetch` requests that do not set a `cache` option to `'no-store'` but leaves `fetch` requests that opt into `'force-cache'` or use a positive `revalidate` as is.
94	- **`number`**: (in seconds) Set the default revalidation frequency of a layout or page to `n` seconds.
95	
96	> **Good to know**:
97	>
98	> - The revalidate value needs to be statically analyzable. For example `revalidate = 600` is valid, but `revalidate = 60 * 10` is not.
99	> - The revalidate value is not available when using `runtime = 'edge'`.
100	> - In Development, Pages are _always_ rendered on-demand and are never cached. This allows you to see changes immediately without waiting for a revalidation period to pass.

4	---
5	
6	The `useRouter` hook allows you to programmatically change routes inside [Client Components](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components).
7	
8	> **Recommendation:** Use the [`<Link>` component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link) for navigation unless you have a specific requirement for using `useRouter`.
9	
10	```tsx filename="app/example-client-component.tsx" switcher
11	'use client'
12	
13	import { useRouter } from 'next/navigation'
14	
15	export default function Page() {
16	  const router = useRouter()
17	
18	  return (
19	    <button type="button" onClick={() => router.push('/dashboard')}>
20	      Dashboard
21	    </button>
22	  )
23	}
24	```
25	
26	```jsx filename="app/example-client-component.js" switcher
27	'use client'
28	
29	import { useRouter } from 'next/navigation'
30	
31	export default function Page() {
32	  const router = useRouter()
33	
34	  return (
35	    <button type="button" onClick={() => router.push('/dashboard')}>
36	      Dashboard
37	    </button>
38	  )
39	}
40	```
41	
42	## `useRouter()`
43	
44	- `router.push(href: string, { scroll: boolean })`: Perform a client-side navigation to the provided route. Adds a new entry into the [browser's history stack](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/History_API).
45	- `router.replace(href: string, { scroll: boolean })`: Perform a client-side navigation to the provided route without adding a new entry into the browser’s history stack.
46	- `router.refresh()`: Refresh the current route. Making a new request to the server, re-fetching data requests, and re-rendering Server Components. The client will merge the updated React Server Component payload without losing unaffected client-side React (e.g. `useState`) or browser state (e.g. scroll position).
47	- `router.prefetch(href: string, options?: { onInvalidate?: () => void })`: [Prefetch](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#prefetching) the provided route for faster client-side transitions. The optional `onInvalidate` callback is called when the [prefetched data becomes stale](/docs/app/guides/prefetching#extending-or-ejecting-link).
48	- `router.back()`: Navigate back to the previous route in the browser’s history stack.
49	- `router.forward()`: Navigate forwards to the next page in the browser’s history stack.
50	
51	> **Good to know**:
52	>
53	> - You must not send untrusted or unsanitized URLs to `router.push` or `router.replace`, as this can open your site to cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities. For example, `javascript:` URLs sent to `router.push` or `router.replace` will be executed in the context of your page.
54	> - The `<Link>` component automatically prefetch routes as they become visible in the viewport.
55	> - `refresh()` could re-produce the same result if fetch requests are cached. Other Dynamic APIs like `cookies` and `headers` could also change the response.
56	> - The `onInvalidate` callback is called at most once per prefetch request. It signals when you may want to trigger a new prefetch for updated route data.
57	
58	### Migrating from `next/router`
59	
60	- The `useRouter` hook should be imported from `next/navigation` and not `next/router` when using the App Router
61	- The `pathname` string has been removed and is replaced by [`usePathname()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-pathname)
62	- The `query` object has been removed and is replaced by [`useSearchParams()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-search-params)
63	- `router.events` has been replaced. [See below.](#router-events)
64	
65	[View the full migration guide](/docs/app/guides/migrating/app-router-migration).
66
